title: Minefield of AI tax returns
full_text:
Desperate Aussies are turning to artificial intelligence (AI) to squeeze more money from their tax returns, but the “gamble” risks putting them in the crosshairs of the ATO, new research has warned.
A national survey by Compare Club reveals that 36.8 per cent of working Australians are using or planning to use AI to prepare their tax returns, with 9.5 per cent already ditching any help from tax professionals to rely solely on tools like ChatGPT.
The data has also uncovered cost-of-living issues with 41.3 per cent of respondents having spent or partially spent their expected refund before even lodging a return, and 47.2 per cent say a refund is now essential to the household budget.
“AI tax agents have officially arrived, but Australians are treading a fine line,” Compare Club’s head of research, Kate Browne, said.
“They are turning to platforms like ChatGPT out of desperation to find missed deductions, despite concerns the technology might give them inaccurate information that could get them into trouble with the ATO. They are gambling on making a saving of a few hundred dollars by not using a professional to do their tax return, but at the same time fear missing out on deductions in the thousands.
“It really is a minefield.” Ms Browne said the research underscored the desire of taxpayers to minimise costs associated with engaging financial experts to assist with returns. “The financial year hasn’t even ended, so it was a shock to learn (how many respondents) have already, fully or partially, allocated an anticipated refund to expenses,” she said.
“For 36,1 per cent of people, any refund will be used to pay bills, while 31.6 per cent are planning to use it to pay down debt. This data shows that for millions of Australians, tax refunds are no longer a bonus, they’re a financial necessity.
“The days of putting tax returns towards a new big screen TV or holiday are long gone. People are depending on this money to help them pay bills and reduce their debt levels.” ATO Assistant Commissioner Anita Challen warns Australians to think twice before acting on information from third-party sources such as AI platforms.
“In an environment where misinformation can spread … it’s important to pause and check your tax information before you act on it,” she said.
“For example, when you go to lodge your tax return … if a tax claim sounds too good to be true, it’s worth checking … AI can be helpful, but it often draws from a broad and inconsistent range of sources, which can lead to inaccurate advice. “Tax misinformation often sounds convincing, but dodgy tax advice can also lead to significant penalties,” she said. “Taxpayers remain accountable for ensuring the information they or their agents provide to the ATO is accurate – whether the advice came from a friend, online sources, or if AI tools were used in its preparation.”

author: Peter Jenkins
heading: Data Science and AI
